original_abstract: In this paper, we train a semantic parser that scales up to Freebase. Instead of relying on annotated logical forms, which is especially expensive to obtain at large scale, we learn from question-answer pairs. The main challenge in this setting is narrowing down the huge number of possible logical predicates for a given question. We tackle this problem in two ways: First, we build a coarse mapping from phrases to predicates using a knowledge base and a large text corpus. Second, we use a bridging operation to generate additional predicates based on neighboring predicates. On the dataset of Cai and Yates (2013), despite not having annotated logical forms, our system outperforms their state-of-the-art parser. Additionally, we collected a more realistic and challenging dataset of question-answer pairs and improves over a natural baseline.
humanized_abstract: We present a semantic parser that can handle the scale of Freebase. Instead of relying on expensive annotations of logical forms, we train the parser from question-answer pairs. The main challenge is to identify a reasonable set of logical predicates for each question that the parser can choose from. We address this challenge with two techniques. First, we learn a coarse mapping between phrases and predicates using Freebase and a large text corpus. Second, we train a special bridging operation to generate additional predicates that connect neighboring predicates. Without access to the annotated logical forms (which is a costly resource), our parser still achieves a better performance on the Cai and Yates dataset than the state-of-the-art parser. We also build a new dataset of question-answer pairs that are more human-behavior-driven and more challenging to tackle, and we show that our parser also has a better performance than a simple natural baseline.

Without access to the annotated logical forms (which is a costly resource), our parser still achieves a better performance on the Cai and Yates dataset than the state-of-the-art parser. We also build a new dataset of question-answer pairs that are more human-behavior-driven and more challenging to tackle, and we show that our parser also has a better performance than a simple natural baseline.
